text: The robotic arm lowers the hepatic dome to expose the supra-hepatic veins for dissection. The cholestectomy is completed, with the cystic artery and duct sectioned between Hem-o-lok clips. The hepatic pedicle is dissected, and the robotic arm aids in exposing the hepatic arterial branches, including the left arterial branch and the branch for segment four, which are sectioned between Hem-o-lok clips to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the left portal branch, securing it with hemlock clips before sectioning. The inferior vena cava is released, and the right part of the caudate lobe is sectioned. A fold is used to aid in exposure.

The surgeon sections accessory infra-hepatic veins using hemlock clips. A parenchymal transection line is marked with monopolar coagulation on the anterior sector of the right side of the medial hepatic vein, guided by ultrasonographic analysis. Parenchymal transection is performed using a robotic hook, bipolar forceps, ultrasonic dissector, and vacuum irrigator. The irrigation enhances bipolar energy efficiency and prevents tissue adhesion. A segment V vein draining into the median hepatic vein is sectioned with hemlock clips. Tumoral resection necessitates extending the parenchymal resection in the anterior sector.

The surgeon identifies the left hepatic duct, using indocyanine green injection to facilitate visualization of biliary convergence. The left hepatic duct is dissected and controlled with hematoclips to prevent right biliary injury. The tumor is removed from the upper part of the portal division, while the caudate lobe is dissected and the inferior vena cava is released. The anterior part of the inferior vena cava is exposed using a robotic arm.

The surgeon uses robotic bipolar and hook instruments to control and dissect accessory suprahepatic veins. Two bipolar forceps are employed to enhance exposure and ensure hemostasis along the transection line. The venous branch from segment VIII, draining into the median hepatic vein, is sectioned between hemlock clips. The inferior vena cava becomes visible at the end of the parenchymal transection.

The surgeon dissects segment four and the superior part of segment one from the inferior vena cava. The left part of the retroclobal ligament is controlled, coagulated, and sectioned using surgical instruments.

The surgeon controls and sections the median hepatic vein using hematoclips, with a loop facilitating exposure. The left hepatic vein is exposed and sectioned using a vascular stapler. The left triangular ligament is sectioned, and the specimen is removed through a financial incision. The transection line is visible at the end of the operation.
